seoTitle: Product Manager or Product Owner: prioritization, AI, ceremonies | Luc Rousseau
seoDescription: PM or PO need without full-time: priorities, prerequisites, prototyping, and dev ceremonies — about four days per week in dense fractional support.

hero.title: PM or PO need, without full-time
hero.lede:
You need a PM or PO, without a 40-hour hire. The team ships code; what's missing is quarterly prioritization, clear prerequisites, dev rituals, and hypotheses tested before build. It's not « too early for a PM » — it's too heavy or too rigid full-time.

That fits as dense fractional support: about four days per week, often as half-days (stand-up in the morning, backlog or prerequisites in the afternoon), plus full days to evaluate, prototype, or validate before the dev team commits.

blocks.title: When developers wait on product decisions
blocks.lede: Without a PM or PO, the founder arbitrates between customer fires, tech debt, and « the next big feature ». Developers move tickets, but the why and the order stay implicit. Each quarter, the roadmap looks like a wish list — not a plan the team can hold.

items.title: About four days per week, as half-days
items.content: Stand-up in the morning, backlog review in the afternoon, prerequisite workshops, quarter sync — the rhythm fits your team calendar. Four days of product presence, split into half-days when that's more efficient, without hiring a fixed fifth day in-house.

items.title: Full days when you need to go deeper
items.content: When several hypotheses or features must be evaluated, prototyped, or tested before build, we stack full days: AI prototypes, clickable flows, light POCs — to validate fast, not to replace production delivery.

items.title: Not an employee — a senior partner
items.content: Same person for prioritization, prerequisites, and technical judgment — no handoff between « the PM » and « someone who gets the code ». Scope and rhythm adjustable without hiring or layoffs.

blocks.title: Four responsibilities a strong PM/PO owns

items.title: Quarterly prioritization
items.content: Quarter goals, explicit trade-offs, what you won't do as much as what you will. A review rhythm (monthly or quarterly) the team understands and the business can defend.

items.title: Prerequisites and ready-to-build
items.content: User stories, acceptance criteria, dependencies, open questions — before devs spend days on ambiguity. Fewer loops, fewer « we misunderstood » at sprint end.

items.title: AI prototypes before full build
items.content: Someone comfortable with AI who ships fast prototypes (flows, interactive mocks, light POCs) to validate hypotheses and test functionality with users — before committing weeks of development.

items.title: Daily ceremonies with developers
items.content: Stand-up, unblocking, sprint alignment: someone who facilitates and keeps rituals on track, follows impediments, and connects product priority to what's happening in code.

blocks.title: Full-time PM/PO vs dense fractional support
blocks.intro: Ballpark — exact calendar (half-days vs full days) is set on a call.

headers.fractionalLead: Senior Product Engineer (~4 days / week, often as half-days + full days as needed)

rows.fullTimePm: 5 days; fixed role even in quieter weeks.
rows.fractionalLead: ~4 days per week (often as half-days); full days when deep prototyping or validation is needed.

rows.fullTimePm: Varies; frequent handoffs with the dev team.
rows.fractionalLead: Same person for prioritization, prerequisites, and realistic technical trade-offs.

rows.fullTimePm: Often Figma mocks; few executable POCs without dev time.
rows.fractionalLead: AI prototypes and POCs on dedicated full days — before committing build.

rows.fullTimePm: Salary + benefits; costly to reposition if the product pivots.
rows.fractionalLead: Fractional budget; intensity scaled up or down without layoffs.
Half-days aligned to stand-up and key rituals; async follow-up between sessions.

items.title: We already have an « implicit » head of product (the founder)
items.content: Common — and draining. My role is to structure what the founder already carries: backlog, quarter, prerequisites, rituals — so you keep vision without being the bottleneck every morning.

items.title: Why half-days instead of full days?
items.content: To match real rhythm: stand-up and unblocking in the morning, prerequisite or prioritization workshops in the afternoon — without paying a full day when a half-day is enough. Full days come when validation or prototyping volume warrants it.

items.title: Four days a week — isn't that almost full-time?
items.content: It's substantial product capacity — on purpose, because the need is real. Versus an employee: no employer costs, flexible calendar (half-days vs full days), and ability to adjust intensity by phase (discovery vs steady execution).

items.title: AI prototypes aren't « real » product
items.content: Exactly: the goal is to reduce risk before code. A clickable flow or minimal POC lets you say no early, yes with confidence — not replace production delivery.

items.title: What if we hire a PM/PO later?
items.content: Even better if you arrive with roadmap, rituals, and ready-to-build standards already proven. I can help define the role and hand off to the internal hire.

blocks.label: Discuss your PM/PO need
blocks.teaser: Need a PM without full-time? A 30-minute call is enough to see if about four days per week — as half-days, with full days for prototyping — fits your team.
